enrichment.short_description: Bolt is a US-based fintech company that provides one-click checkout and payment infrastructure for online merchants. It is unrelated to Bolt (formerly Taxify), the Estonia-based ride-hailing and mobility super-app at bolt.eu.
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 300-500 employees (headcount has fluctuated with layoffs since 2022)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bolt is a US fintech offering one-click checkout for online merchants. See what Bolt.com does, its funding history, and how it differs from ride-hailing app Bolt.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bolt Financial Inc., operating at bolt.com, is an American financial technology company founded in 2014 in San Francisco by Stanford students Ryan Breslow and Eric Feldman. Its core product, Bolt Checkout, lets e-commerce merchants offer shoppers a one-click purchase experience by reusing stored payment and shipping details across a shared shopper network, with the stated goal of reducing cart abandonment and helping independent retailers compete with Amazon-style checkout speed. Bolt should not be confused with Bolt (formerly Taxify), the unrelated Estonia-based ride-hailing and micromobility super-app that operates at bolt.eu across Europe and Africa; that company has no connection to Bolt Financial. Bolt Financial has raised roughly 1.6 billion dollars in total funding. Its most notable round was a 355 million dollar Series E in January 2022 that valued the company at around 11 billion dollars, making it a decacorn. Six days after that round closed, founder Ryan Breslow posted a Twitter thread criticizing Sequoia Capital and Y Combinator, then stepped down as CEO to become executive chairman; COO Maju Kuruvilla took over as CEO. In mid-2023, reports surfaced that the SEC was investigating whether Breslow made misleading statements to investors during the Series E fundraise, and some Series E investors alleged the round valued the company on inflated projections. Bolt's valuation subsequently became a point of dispute: some investor marks reportedly cut the company's valuation by roughly 97 percent to around 300 million dollars by early 2024, while other 2024 financing activity, including a 220 million dollar debt round and a reported 450 million dollar raise later that year, was associated with valuations cited anywhere from 11 billion to 14 billion dollars depending on the source. In March 2025, Ryan Breslow returned as CEO, and the company has since been reported on for aggressive internal restructuring, including cutting its HR department. Bolt remains headquartered in San Francisco and continues to sell checkout, fraud-prevention, and payments infrastructure to online merchants on a custom, enterprise-priced basis rather than published self-serve pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bolt
paragraphs: Bolt (bolt.com), formally Bolt Financial Inc., is a US fintech company that builds one-click checkout and payments infrastructure for online merchants. Founded in 2014 in San Francisco by Ryan Breslow and Eric Feldman, the company set out to give independent retailers a fast checkout experience comparable to Amazon, using a shared shopper network so returning customers can complete purchases in a single click across any merchant running Bolt.

It is important not to confuse this Bolt with Bolt (formerly Taxify), a completely separate and unrelated company headquartered in Tallinn, Estonia, that operates a ride-hailing, food delivery, and micromobility super-app at bolt.eu across Europe and Africa. The two companies share only a name; Bolt Financial has never been involved in ride-hailing.

Bolt Financial has gone through significant public turbulence. Its January 2022 Series E raised 355 million dollars at close to an 11 billion dollar valuation, briefly making it one of fintech's most valuable startups, but was quickly followed by founder Ryan Breslow's public dispute with investors Sequoia Capital and Y Combinator, his resignation as CEO, an SEC inquiry into Series E fundraising statements, and sharply conflicting valuation marks in 2024. Breslow returned as CEO in March 2025, and the company continues operating as a merchant checkout provider today.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bolt's core product is its one-click checkout widget, which merchants embed into their online store so that any shopper already in the Bolt network can check out without re-entering payment or shipping information. The network effect is central to the pitch: the more merchants use Bolt, the more shoppers already have stored profiles, and the faster checkout becomes across every store on the network.

Beyond checkout, Bolt offers fraud detection tooling built on machine learning models trained across its merchant network, along with chargeback protection options, merchant analytics, and integrations with major commerce platforms and custom APIs for larger enterprise merchants building bespoke storefronts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bolt does not publish self-serve pricing. As a business-to-business fintech platform, it sells to merchants through custom enterprise contracts that typically factor in transaction volume, integration complexity, and the specific mix of checkout, fraud, and payments products a merchant adopts.
Prospective merchants generally need to contact Bolt's sales team for a quote, and pricing is not standardized into published tiers the way many self-serve SaaS products are.

features.title: One-Click Checkout
features.description: Lets returning shoppers on any Bolt-enabled merchant site complete a purchase using stored payment and shipping details in a single click.

features.title: Shopper Network
features.description: Aggregates shopper profiles across Bolt merchants so checkout gets faster the more stores a shopper has purchased from.

features.title: Fraud Detection
features.description: Machine learning-based fraud screening trained on transaction data across the merchant network, aimed at reducing chargebacks.

features.title: Merchant Integrations
features.description: Connects with major e-commerce platforms and offers custom APIs for enterprise merchants with bespoke storefronts.

features.title: Checkout Analytics
features.description: Gives merchants visibility into conversion rates, cart abandonment, and checkout performance.

features.title: Mobile-Optimized Flow
features.description: Checkout experience designed to minimize friction and steps on mobile devices.

features.title: Stored Payment Credentials
features.description: Securely stores shopper payment methods for reuse across future purchases.

features.title: Enterprise Support
features.description: Dedicated implementation and account support for large merchant integrations.

pros: Reduces checkout steps and can meaningfully cut cart abandonment for merchants
Shared shopper network makes checkout faster the more merchants adopt it
Includes built-in fraud detection alongside checkout
Backed by a long operating history since 2014 and deep e-commerce integrations
Positioned to help independent retailers compete with large platforms on checkout speed
cons: No public self-serve pricing, requiring a sales conversation for any merchant
Company has experienced significant leadership instability, including an SEC inquiry into its 2022 fundraising
Reported valuation has been highly volatile and inconsistently marked across sources since 2022
Primarily suited to merchants with meaningful transaction volume rather than very small sellers
Frequently confused with the unrelated European ride-hailing company of the same name

faqs.question: Is Bolt (bolt.com) the same company as the ride-hailing app Bolt?
faqs.answer: No. Bolt.com is Bolt Financial Inc., a US checkout and payments fintech founded in 2014. The ride-hailing and mobility app called Bolt, formerly Taxify, is a separate, unrelated company based in Tallinn, Estonia, at bolt.eu.

faqs.question: What does Bolt do?
faqs.answer: Bolt provides one-click checkout, fraud detection, and payments infrastructure that online merchants embed into their stores to speed up purchase completion and reduce cart abandonment.

faqs.question: Who founded Bolt?
faqs.answer: Bolt was founded in 2014 in San Francisco by Ryan Breslow and Eric Feldman.

faqs.question: How much funding has Bolt raised?
faqs.answer: Bolt has raised approximately 1.6 billion dollars in total funding, including a 355 million dollar Series E in January 2022 that briefly valued it at around 11 billion dollars.

faqs.question: What happened with Bolt's CEO Ryan Breslow?
faqs.answer: Breslow stepped down as CEO in early 2022 shortly after a public dispute with investors, was investigated by the SEC over statements made during the Series E fundraise, and returned to the CEO role in March 2025.

faqs.question: Is Bolt still operating?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bolt continues to operate as a merchant checkout and payments provider despite valuation volatility and leadership changes since 2022.

faqs.question: Is Bolt publicly traded?
faqs.answer: No. Bolt is a privately held company backed by venture capital and debt financing.

faqs.question: What platforms does Bolt integrate with?
faqs.answer: Bolt integrates with major e-commerce platforms and offers custom APIs for enterprise merchants building their own storefronts.

use_cases.title: Reducing cart abandonment
use_cases.description: Online retailers embed Bolt's one-click checkout to shorten the path to purchase and recover sales that would otherwise be lost to a long checkout flow.

use_cases.title: Competing with large platforms on checkout speed
use_cases.description: Enterprise retailers use Bolt to match the fast, low-friction checkout experience shoppers expect from dominant marketplaces.

use_cases.title: Shared fraud intelligence
use_cases.description: Risk and payments teams use Bolt's network-trained fraud detection to catch fraudulent transactions before they result in chargebacks.

enrichment.short_description: Bomi, formerly known as CMPlayer, is a free and open-source graphical media player built on the mpv playback engine, historically available for Linux and, in earlier versions, macOS.
enrichment.seo_title: Bomi Player: Free Open-Source mpv Media Player
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bomi is a free, open-source GUI media player built on mpv. Explore its playback history, subtitle support, hardware decoding, and platform availability.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bomi is a free and open-source graphical multimedia player built on top of the mpv playback engine, originally released under the name CMPlayer around 2008 by South Korean developer Lee Byoung-young, known online as xylosper, before being renamed to Bomi around 2014 to 2015. It is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2, with source code hosted on GitHub, and its project page lives at bomi-player.github.io. Bomi wraps mpv's playback engine in a full graphical interface aimed at users who want mpv's power without relying purely on the command line. Notable capabilities include automatic playback history tracking with the ability to resume any file from where playback stopped, automatic playlist generation and restoration of the last-viewed list, and advanced subtitle handling that supports multiple simultaneous subtitle tracks in a wide range of formats, including styled formats like SAMI and ASS. Bomi also supports GPU-accelerated video decoding through VA-API and VDPAU on Linux, extensive customization of its interface, keyboard and mouse gesture controls, and a multilingual interface available in English, Korean, and Japanese, reflecting its Korean origins. Bomi's primary and best-supported platform is Linux; the software did have experimental macOS (then OS X) support in earlier releases, but that support was disabled after roughly version 0.8.17 when the developer's own Mac hardware broke, and macOS builds have not been an officially maintained part of the project since. There is no confirmed evidence of an actively maintained native Windows build being central to the project either, based on available project documentation. Development activity appears to have slowed significantly in the years since the project's most active period, though the software remains downloadable and usable for existing installations. Because it is completely free and open source with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees, Bomi is best understood as a community-maintained alternative to players like VLC or a bare mpv installation, aimed at Linux users who want mpv's playback quality wrapped in a more approachable, configurable graphical shell.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bomi Player
paragraphs: Bomi is a free, open-source graphical media player built on top of the mpv playback engine. It began life around 2008 as CMPlayer, created by South Korean developer Lee Byoung-young (xylosper), and was renamed Bomi around 2014 to 2015. The project is licensed under GPLv2 and hosted on GitHub, with its documentation and downloads available at bomi-player.github.io.

Rather than reinventing video decoding, Bomi focuses on wrapping the well-regarded mpv engine in a full desktop GUI, adding conveniences like automatic history tracking, playlist management, and rich subtitle handling on top of mpv's playback quality.

Bomi's primary and best-supported platform is Linux. It did include macOS support in earlier releases, but that support was disabled after version 0.8.17 and has not been officially maintained since, so users on macOS today would rely on legacy builds rather than current, supported releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bomi records playback history automatically and lets users resume any file exactly where they left off, alongside remembering audio track and subtitle selections per file. It also auto-generates playlists and can restore the last-played list on startup.

Subtitle handling is one of Bomi's strongest areas: it can render multiple subtitle files simultaneously and supports styled formats such as SAMI and ASS, not just plain SRT files. On Linux, it takes advantage of hardware-accelerated decoding through VA-API and VDPAU where available, and its interface, including keyboard and mouse gestures, can be extensively customized. The interface itself is available in English, Korean, and Japanese.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bomi is completely free and open source under the GPLv2 license. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions, license keys, or premium feature gates of any kind.
Anyone can download, use, modify, and redistribute Bomi's source code under the terms of the GPL, consistent with its origins as a community-driven project.

features.title: mpv-Powered Playback
features.description: Uses the mpv engine under the hood for high-quality audio and video decoding wrapped in a full graphical interface.

features.title: Playback History and Resume
features.description: Automatically tracks watched files and lets users resume playback from where they stopped, including remembered audio and subtitle selections.

features.title: Advanced Subtitle Support
features.description: Renders multiple subtitle tracks simultaneously and supports styled formats such as SAMI and ASS in addition to standard SRT.

features.title: Hardware-Accelerated Decoding
features.description: Supports GPU-accelerated decoding via VA-API and VDPAU on Linux systems.

features.title: Customizable Interface and Gestures
features.description: Allows deep customization of the interface along with configurable keyboard and mouse gesture controls.

features.title: Automatic Playlist Generation
features.description: Builds playlists automatically from a media folder and restores the last-played list on startup.

features.title: Multilingual Interface
features.description: Available in English, Korean, and Japanese, reflecting the project's Korean origins.

features.title: Free and Open Source
features.description: Distributed under GPLv2 with source code openly available on GitHub, with no cost to use.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Built on the powerful and well-regarded mpv playback engine
Extensive, best-in-class subtitle format and multi-track support
Deep interface customization including keyboard and mouse gestures
Lightweight compared to many full-featured media center applications
cons: Primarily a Linux application, with macOS support discontinued after early versions
Development activity appears to have slowed considerably in recent years
Smaller community and less frequent updates compared to VLC or mpv itself
Advanced configuration options can be overwhelming for casual users
Documentation is sparser than more mainstream media player projects

faqs.question: What is Bomi Player?
faqs.answer: Bomi is a free, open-source graphical media player built on the mpv playback engine, originally released as CMPlayer around 2008 and renamed Bomi around 2014 to 2015.

faqs.question: Is Bomi free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bomi is distributed under the GPLv2 open-source license with no cost, subscriptions, or paid tiers.

faqs.question: Does Bomi support macOS?
faqs.answer: Bomi had macOS support in earlier versions up through around release 0.8.17, but that support was disabled afterward and has not been an officially maintained part of the project since.

faqs.question: What is Bomi built on?
faqs.answer: Bomi is built on top of the mpv media playback engine, adding a full graphical interface, playback history, and enhanced subtitle handling.

faqs.question: Was Bomi previously known by a different name?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bomi was originally released as CMPlayer before being renamed to Bomi around 2014 to 2015.

faqs.question: Is Bomi still actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Development activity has slowed considerably in recent years compared to the project's earlier active period, though the software remains available for download and use.

faqs.question: What subtitle formats does Bomi support?
faqs.answer: Bomi supports a wide range of subtitle formats, including plain SRT as well as styled formats like SAMI and ASS, and can display multiple subtitle tracks at once.

faqs.question: How is Bomi different from plain mpv or VLC?
faqs.answer: Bomi wraps the mpv engine in a full graphical interface with features like automatic playback history and advanced subtitle handling, positioning it between bare mpv and more mainstream players like VLC.

use_cases.title: Linux desktop media playback
use_cases.description: Linux users install Bomi as a graphical front end to mpv for everyday video and audio playback.

use_cases.title: Managing large local media libraries
use_cases.description: Viewers with extensive local video collections use Bomi's automatic history and resume features to pick up playback across many files.

use_cases.title: Multi-subtitle viewing
use_cases.description: Users who need to display multiple subtitle tracks or styled subtitle formats at once rely on Bomi's advanced subtitle rendering.
use_cases.audience: Language learners and multi-audio content viewers

enrichment.short_description: Bonsai, also known as Hello Bonsai, is an all-in-one business management platform for freelancers and small agencies that combines proposals, contracts, invoicing, time tracking, and CRM. It is a completely different product from bonsai.io, the Elasticsearch and OpenSearch hosting service listed separately.
enrichment.company_size: Small-to-midsize startup team; Y Combinator W16 alumnus
enrichment.seo_title: Hello Bonsai Review 2026: Freelancer Business Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bonsai (Hello Bonsai) is all-in-one software for freelancers: contracts, invoicing, proposals, and time tracking. See pricing, features, and pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bonsai, marketed as Hello Bonsai at hellobonsai.com, is an all-in-one business management platform built for freelancers and small agencies. It was founded in 2015 and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2016 batch, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. The platform reports serving more than 500,000 freelancers and agencies worldwide. It is important to distinguish Hello Bonsai from bonsai.io, a completely separate and unrelated company that provides managed Elasticsearch and OpenSearch hosting for developers; the two products share only a name and have no corporate relationship. Hello Bonsai's core value proposition is consolidating the back-office tools a freelancer or small agency needs into one product: customizable proposal and contract templates with e-signature support, invoicing and online payment collection, an in-app time tracker for billable and non-billable hours, a lightweight CRM for storing client contacts and project history, a client portal, task and project management including a Gantt view on higher tiers, expense tracking, and integrations with tools like QuickBooks, Zapier, Calendly, and Google. Pricing is structured across four tiers billed per user per month: Basic at 15 dollars monthly or 9 dollars annually, Essentials at 25 dollars monthly or 19 dollars annually, Premium at 39 dollars monthly or 29 dollars annually, and Elite at 59 dollars monthly or 49 dollars annually, with Elite requiring a minimum of three users and including dedicated onboarding for teams of ten or more. All plans include a 7-day free trial, though Bonsai does not offer a permanent free-forever tier. Reviews commonly praise Bonsai's clean interface, breadth of industry-specific templates, and fast onboarding, while some recent user reviews report delays of seven to ten business days on initial payment payouts. Overall, Hello Bonsai is positioned as a back-office operating system for solo freelancers and small creative or consulting agencies who want proposals, contracts, invoicing, time tracking, and client management under one roof rather than stitched together from separate point tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bonsai (Hello Bonsai)
paragraphs: Bonsai, marketed under the name Hello Bonsai at hellobonsai.com, is an all-in-one business management platform for freelancers and small agencies. It was founded in 2015, went through Y Combinator's Winter 2016 cohort, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and now reports serving more than 500,000 freelancers and agencies.

Hello Bonsai should not be confused with bonsai.io, a separate and unrelated company covered elsewhere in this directory that provides managed Elasticsearch and OpenSearch hosting for developers. The two products have no corporate relationship beyond sharing a similar name.

The platform aims to be a back-office operating system for independent professionals, combining client-facing documents like proposals and contracts with operational tools like invoicing, time tracking, and a lightweight CRM in a single subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hello Bonsai offers customizable proposal and contract templates with built-in e-signature support, invoicing with online payment collection and recurring billing, and an in-app timer for tracking billable and non-billable hours. A built-in CRM keeps client contact details, project history, and communication together.

Higher tiers add project and task management with a Gantt view, a client portal, expense tracking, custom fields, workload and profitability reporting, and integrations with QuickBooks, Zapier, Calendly, and Google, letting agencies extend Bonsai into their broader toolchain.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hello Bonsai uses per-user, per-month pricing across four tiers: Basic (15 dollars monthly, 9 dollars annually), Essentials (25 dollars monthly, 19 dollars annually), Premium (39 dollars monthly, 29 dollars annually), and Elite (59 dollars monthly, 49 dollars annually, with a three-user minimum).
There is no permanent free plan, but every tier includes a 7-day free trial with full feature access, and users can change plans at any time as their business needs grow.

features.title: Proposals and Contracts
features.description: Customizable, industry-specific proposal and contract templates with built-in e-signature capability.

features.title: Invoicing and Payments
features.description: Create and send invoices, collect online payments, and automate recurring billing.

features.title: Time Tracking
features.description: An in-app timer records billable and non-billable hours against clients and projects.

features.title: Built-In CRM
features.description: Stores client contact details, project history, and communication in one place.

features.title: Client Portal
features.description: Gives clients a dedicated space to view proposals, contracts, invoices, and project updates.

features.title: Project and Task Management
features.description: Task tracking with a Gantt view and workload management available on Premium and Elite tiers.

features.title: Expense Tracking
features.description: Logs business expenses and, on higher tiers, supports expense markups billed to clients.

features.title: Third-Party Integrations
features.description: Connects with QuickBooks, Zapier, Calendly, Google, and Xero depending on plan tier.

pros: Consolidates proposals, contracts, invoicing, time tracking, and CRM into one subscription
Professional, industry-specific templates speed up client onboarding
Clean, visually clear interface with a relatively fast learning curve
Mobile apps for iOS and Android support on-the-go invoicing and time tracking
Tiered plans let solo freelancers and growing agencies pick the feature depth they need
cons: Per-user pricing can get expensive for agencies with larger teams
No permanent free plan, only a 7-day trial
Some recent user reviews report delays of seven to ten business days on initial payment payouts
Advanced features like Gantt views and custom permissions are gated behind higher tiers
Deep integrations such as Xero are limited to the top Elite plan

faqs.question: What is Hello Bonsai used for?
faqs.answer: Hello Bonsai is business management software for freelancers and small agencies, covering proposals, contracts, invoicing, time tracking, and client relationship management.

faqs.question: Is Bonsai the same company as bonsai.io?
faqs.answer: No. Hello Bonsai is a freelancer business management platform, while bonsai.io is a completely separate and unrelated company offering managed Elasticsearch and OpenSearch hosting for developers.

faqs.question: Does Hello Bonsai have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No permanent free plan is offered, but every paid tier includes a 7-day free trial with full access.

faqs.question: What is Hello Bonsai's starting price?
faqs.answer: The Basic plan starts at 15 dollars per user per month billed monthly, or 9 dollars per user per month billed annually.

faqs.question: Can Hello Bonsai handle client payments?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bonsai supports invoicing with online payment collection starting on the Essentials plan.

faqs.question: Who is Hello Bonsai best for?
faqs.answer: It is best suited to solo freelancers, consultants, and small creative or marketing agencies that want an all-in-one back-office tool.

faqs.question: Does Hello Bonsai integrate with QuickBooks?
faqs.answer: Yes, QuickBooks integration is available starting on the Premium plan.

faqs.question: Is there a Hello Bonsai mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bonsai offers mobile apps for both iOS and Android.

use_cases.title: Managing freelance clients end to end
use_cases.description: Solo freelancers use Bonsai to send proposals, sign contracts, track time, and invoice clients from a single platform.

use_cases.title: Standardizing agency operations
use_cases.description: Small creative or marketing agencies use Bonsai's templates and CRM to keep proposals, contracts, and client communication consistent across the team.

use_cases.title: Tracking billable time and invoicing
use_cases.description: Consultants use Bonsai's in-app timer and invoicing tools to bill clients accurately for hours worked.

enrichment.short_description: Bonsai, at bonsai.io, is a fully managed Elasticsearch and OpenSearch hosting platform for developers. Despite the similar name, it is a completely different, unrelated product from Hello Bonsai, the freelancer business-management tool covered separately in this directory.
enrichment.starting_price: Free Sandbox tier; paid plans from around $15-25/month
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bonsai.io offers fully managed Elasticsearch and OpenSearch hosting. See pricing, features, and how it differs from the unrelated freelancer tool Hello Bonsai.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bonsai, hosted at bonsai.io, is a fully managed Elasticsearch and OpenSearch hosting platform built for developers and engineering teams. It is developed by One More Cloud, a small, bootstrapped, self-funded software company based in Austin, Texas, founded around 2009, with the Bonsai search product itself launching in 2012 as one of the first hosted Elasticsearch-as-a-service offerings on the market. In 2021 Bonsai became one of the first providers to offer managed OpenSearch alongside Elasticsearch, following the Elasticsearch and OpenSearch licensing split. It is critical to note that this bonsai.io product has no relationship whatsoever to Hello Bonsai, the freelancer business-management platform covered elsewhere in this directory at hellobonsai.com; the two companies were founded independently, serve entirely different markets, and only share a similar brand name. Bonsai.io reports managing more than 20,000 clusters and serving over one billion requests per day, and it is available as an add-on on platforms like Heroku in addition to its own direct signup. The company's pricing is structured across three broad tiers: a Standard tier ranging roughly from 25 to 400 dollars per month with named sub-plans such as Staging, Fir, Elm, Cork, Teak, and Oak, offering AI-ready vector search, 24 by 7 monitoring, and email support; a Business tier ranging roughly from 600 to 2,500 dollars per month adding a 99.99 percent uptime SLA, dedicated infrastructure, multi-availability-zone hosting, off-site backups, and a one-hour response-time SLA; and an Enterprise tier starting around 5,500 dollars per month that adds expert advisory support, zero-downtime upgrades, 24 by 7 emergency contact, bring-your-own-cloud deployment by default, and compliance features like FIPS-140-2 support. There is also a free Sandbox tier intended for experimentation and small development or research workloads. All plans include encryption, hourly backups, and GDPR and CCPA compliance, while Business and Enterprise plans add SOC 2 and HIPAA business-associate-agreement support. Bonsai.io is positioned as a low-overhead way for teams to run production search infrastructure without operating Elasticsearch or OpenSearch clusters themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bonsai (bonsai.io)
paragraphs: Bonsai, at bonsai.io, is a fully managed hosting platform for Elasticsearch and OpenSearch, built by One More Cloud, a small, bootstrapped, self-funded company based in Austin, Texas. The company was founded around 2009, and the Bonsai search hosting product launched in 2012 as one of the first hosted Elasticsearch-as-a-service offerings available.

This bonsai.io product is entirely separate and unrelated to Hello Bonsai, the freelancer and agency business-management platform also covered in this directory at hellobonsai.com. The two companies were founded independently, target completely different markets, developers versus freelancers, and only happen to share a similar name.

Bonsai.io reports operating more than 20,000 clusters and handling over one billion requests per day, and is available both as a direct signup and as an add-on on platforms such as Heroku. In 2021 it became one of the first providers to offer managed OpenSearch clusters as an alternative to Elasticsearch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bonsai handles cluster provisioning, scaling, and maintenance for both Elasticsearch and OpenSearch, so engineering teams do not need to run their own search infrastructure. It offers a free Sandbox tier for experimentation, and higher paid tiers add dedicated infrastructure, multi-availability-zone hosting, and automated hourly plus off-site backups.

Compliance and reliability features scale with plan tier: all plans include encryption and GDPR and CCPA compliance, Business and Enterprise plans add SOC 2 and HIPAA business-associate-agreement support, and Enterprise adds FIPS-140-2 support, zero-downtime upgrades, and bring-your-own-cloud deployment by default.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bonsai.io's pricing spans three broad tiers. The Standard tier runs roughly 25 to 400 dollars per month across named sub-plans (Staging, Fir, Elm, Cork, Teak, Oak) and suits development, staging, and smaller production workloads. The Business tier runs roughly 600 to 2,500 dollars per month and adds a 99.99 percent uptime SLA and dedicated infrastructure for production-grade needs.

The Enterprise tier starts around 5,500 dollars per month and is aimed at large organizations needing advisory support, zero-downtime upgrades, and stricter compliance guarantees. A free Sandbox plan is also available for testing and small-scale development use, and custom plans can be arranged directly with Bonsai's sales team.

features.title: Fully Managed Elasticsearch and OpenSearch
features.description: Provisions, scales, and maintains Elasticsearch and OpenSearch clusters so teams do not manage search infrastructure themselves.

features.title: Free Sandbox Tier
features.description: A no-cost tier for experimentation, learning, and small development or research workloads.

features.title: Multi-AZ High Availability
features.description: Business and Enterprise plans run dedicated, multi-availability-zone infrastructure for production reliability.

features.title: Automated Backups
features.description: Hourly backups plus off-site backup options protect against data loss.

features.title: Heroku and Platform Add-ons
features.description: Available as an add-on on platforms like Heroku in addition to direct signup.

features.title: Compliance Certifications
features.description: Offers SOC 2, HIPAA business-associate agreements, GDPR and CCPA compliance, and FIPS-140-2 support on Enterprise.

features.title: 24/7 Monitoring and Support
features.description: Continuous cluster monitoring with email support on Standard and faster response-time SLAs on higher tiers.

features.title: Zero-Downtime Upgrades
features.description: Enterprise plans support cluster upgrades without service interruption.

pros: Removes the operational overhead of running Elasticsearch or OpenSearch clusters directly
Free Sandbox tier makes it easy to experiment before committing to a paid plan
Supports both Elasticsearch and OpenSearch, giving flexibility as licensing changes
Strong compliance options, including SOC 2 and HIPAA, for regulated industries
Long operating history as a self-funded, profitable company with Heroku integration
cons: Pricing can climb quickly at Business and Enterprise tiers for larger workloads
Smaller company than hyperscaler-native search offerings from AWS or Elastic Cloud
Highest-tier compliance and reliability features are gated behind Enterprise pricing
Named sub-plan structure within Standard can be confusing to compare at a glance

faqs.question: What is bonsai.io?
faqs.answer: Bonsai.io is a fully managed Elasticsearch and OpenSearch hosting platform for developers, built by Austin, Texas-based company One More Cloud.

faqs.question: Is bonsai.io related to Hello Bonsai, the freelancer tool?
faqs.answer: No. Bonsai.io is a completely separate and unrelated company from Hello Bonsai; they only share a similar name and serve entirely different markets.

faqs.question: Does bonsai.io support OpenSearch?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bonsai became one of the first managed OpenSearch providers in 2021, alongside its existing Elasticsearch hosting.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan on bonsai.io?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bonsai offers a free Sandbox tier suited to experimentation and small development workloads.

faqs.question: Does bonsai.io integrate with Heroku?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bonsai is available as an add-on on Heroku in addition to direct signup on bonsai.io.

faqs.question: Who runs bonsai.io?
faqs.answer: Bonsai.io is developed and operated by One More Cloud, a small, self-funded, profitable company based in Austin, Texas.

faqs.question: What compliance certifications does bonsai.io offer?
faqs.answer: Bonsai offers SOC 2, HIPAA business-associate agreements, GDPR and CCPA compliance on higher tiers, and FIPS-140-2 support on Enterprise.

faqs.question: How does bonsai.io pricing scale?
faqs.answer: Standard plans run roughly 25 to 400 dollars per month, Business plans run roughly 600 to 2,500 dollars per month, and Enterprise starts around 5,500 dollars per month.

use_cases.title: Adding search to an application quickly
use_cases.description: Developers use bonsai.io to get a production-ready Elasticsearch or OpenSearch cluster running without managing infrastructure themselves.

use_cases.title: Meeting compliance requirements for search infrastructure
use_cases.description: Companies in regulated industries use bonsai.io's SOC 2 and HIPAA-eligible plans to run compliant managed search.

use_cases.title: Adding search to a Heroku application
use_cases.description: Heroku-based development teams provision Bonsai directly as an add-on for their existing applications.

features: Named sub-plans from Staging to Oak

enrichment.short_description: Bookmark OS is a visual, browser-based bookmark manager and virtual desktop that lets users organize links, tabs, files, and notes using webpage-screenshot icons and a customizable drag-and-drop desktop.
enrichment.starting_price: Free forever plan; one-time paid plan from $36
enrichment.seo_title: Bookmark OS Review: Visual Bookmark Manager Desktop
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bookmark OS is a visual bookmark manager and virtual desktop for organizing links, tabs, files, and notes. See features, pricing, and who it is for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bookmark OS, at bookmarkos.com, is a browser-based visual bookmark manager and virtual desktop created by web developer and entrepreneur David Lynam and headquartered in Seattle, Washington. Rather than presenting bookmarks as a plain text list, Bookmark OS renders them as visual icons generated from screenshots of the saved web page, and arranges them on a customizable, desktop-like workspace that supports familiar interactions such as drag and drop, drag-to-select, and right-click context menus. Beyond bookmarks, the product combines a tab manager that can save an entire browsing session into a folder, a notes feature for creating documents directly on the desktop, and file storage, positioning itself as a combined bookmark manager, tab manager, task manager, and notes tool in one interface. Additional features include AI-powered folder suggestions that recommend where a new bookmark should be saved, a duplicate bookmark finder to clean up existing collections, and weekly Dropbox backup syncing so a copy of the bookmark library is preserved outside the browser. The product ships with roughly ten selectable visual themes, appealing to a range of users including students, teachers, designers, and developers who want a more visual and spatial way of organizing links than a traditional folder tree. On pricing, Bookmark OS offers a free-forever plan, distinguishing it from purely subscription-based competitors, alongside a paid plan that uses a one-time, perpetual-license payment model starting at 36 dollars rather than a recurring monthly or annual subscription. This one-time pricing approach is relatively uncommon among modern SaaS bookmark tools, most of which charge ongoing subscriptions, and it is one of Bookmark OS's more distinctive positioning points. The tool is browser-based and works as an online workspace accessible from any device with a browser rather than as a native desktop or mobile application.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bookmark OS
paragraphs: Bookmark OS, at bookmarkos.com, is a browser-based visual bookmark manager and virtual desktop created by developer David Lynam and based in Seattle, Washington. Instead of listing saved links as plain text, it displays each bookmark as a visual icon generated from a screenshot of the page, arranged on a customizable, desktop-like workspace.

The product goes beyond bookmarks alone, combining a tab manager for saving entire browsing sessions, a notes feature for creating documents directly on the desktop, and file storage, so users can treat it as a general-purpose visual workspace rather than just a link list.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bookmark OS uses webpage screenshots as bookmark icons for faster visual recognition, offers AI-powered folder suggestions to help sort new bookmarks automatically, and includes a duplicate bookmark finder to clean up cluttered collections. Its tab manager can capture an entire browser session into a dedicated folder for later retrieval.

The interface mimics a desktop operating system, supporting drag-and-drop organization, drag-to-select, and right-click context menus, along with around ten selectable visual themes and weekly Dropbox backup syncing to keep bookmarks safe outside the browser.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bookmark OS offers a free-forever plan for users who want to try the core bookmark and desktop features at no cost. Its paid tier uses a one-time, perpetual-license payment starting at 36 dollars rather than a recurring monthly or annual subscription, which distinguishes it from most competing bookmark tools.

This pricing structure means users pay once for the upgraded plan rather than committing to ongoing billing, which can make the total cost of ownership lower for long-term users compared to subscription-based bookmark managers.

features.title: Visual Bookmark Icons
features.description: Displays each bookmark as an icon generated from a screenshot of the saved web page for faster visual recognition.

features.title: AI Folder Suggestions
features.description: Recommends the correct folder for a new bookmark automatically based on its content.

features.title: Tab Manager and Session Saving
features.description: Saves an entire set of open browser tabs into a session folder for later retrieval.

features.title: Desktop Notes
features.description: Lets users create standalone notes directly on the virtual desktop alongside bookmarks and files.

features.title: Duplicate Bookmark Finder
features.description: Scans the bookmark library to identify and remove duplicate entries.

features.title: Dropbox Backup Sync
features.description: Backs up the bookmark library to Dropbox on a weekly schedule.

features.title: Drag-and-Drop Desktop Interface
features.description: Provides desktop-style interactions including drag and drop, drag-to-select, and right-click menus.

features.title: Customizable Themes
features.description: Offers roughly ten selectable visual themes to personalize the workspace.

pros: Highly visual, screenshot-based organization is easier to scan than plain text bookmark lists
Combines bookmarks, tabs, notes, and files in a single workspace
Offers a genuinely free-forever plan, not just a time-limited trial
One-time payment option available instead of a mandatory recurring subscription
Appeals well to visual learners, students, and designers organizing large numbers of links
cons: Browser-based tool that requires an internet connection to access the workspace
Visual, spatial layout may not suit power users who prefer search-first or folder-tree organization
Smaller, independent team means less frequent updates than large bookmark-tool competitors
Some advanced features are limited to the paid tier
Not a native browser extension replacement, so it exists alongside rather than inside browser bookmarks

faqs.question: What is Bookmark OS?
faqs.answer: Bookmark OS is a browser-based visual bookmark manager and virtual desktop that organizes links, tabs, notes, and files using screenshot-based icons.

faqs.question: Is Bookmark OS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bookmark OS offers a free-forever plan, along with a paid one-time-purchase plan starting at 36 dollars for additional features.

faqs.question: Who makes Bookmark OS?
faqs.answer: Bookmark OS was created by web developer and entrepreneur David Lynam and is based in Seattle, Washington.

faqs.question: Does Bookmark OS work across devices?
faqs.answer: As a browser-based tool, Bookmark OS is accessible from any device with a compatible browser and an internet connection.

faqs.question: Can I back up my bookmarks in Bookmark OS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bookmark OS supports weekly automatic backups to Dropbox.

faqs.question: Does Bookmark OS replace my browser's native bookmarks?
faqs.answer: Bookmark OS operates as a separate visual workspace rather than directly replacing native browser bookmarks, though it can be used as a primary organization system.

faqs.question: Is there a one-time payment option for Bookmark OS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bookmark OS offers a one-time, perpetual-license paid plan starting at 36 dollars in addition to its free plan.

faqs.question: Who is Bookmark OS best for?
faqs.answer: It is best suited to students, researchers, designers, and other users who prefer organizing large numbers of links visually and spatially.

use_cases.title: Organizing research sources visually
use_cases.description: Students and researchers use Bookmark OS to visually organize large numbers of web sources by topic on a virtual desktop.

use_cases.title: Curating visual design inspiration
use_cases.description: Designers use the screenshot-based bookmark icons to build visual mood boards and reference collections.

use_cases.title: Managing project links and notes together
use_cases.description: Professionals use Bookmark OS to keep project-related links, notes, and files in one visual workspace.

enrichment.short_description: Bookworm is a free, open-source ebook manager and reader built with GTK for elementary OS but usable on other Linux distributions, supporting EPUB, MOBI, PDF, and comic book formats.
enrichment.seo_title: Bookworm: Free Open-Source Ebook Reader for Linux
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bookworm is a free, open-source GTK ebook reader and library manager for Linux, supporting EPUB, MOBI, PDF, and comic formats.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bookworm is a free, open-source ebook manager and reader created by developer Siddhartha Das, published under the GitHub username babluboy, and built with the Vala programming language along with GTK and WebKit for native integration on Linux desktops, particularly elementary OS. The source code is hosted at github.com/babluboy/bookworm, with a project page at babluboy.github.io/bookworm, and the application is distributed through Flathub, Snap packages, and Debian and Ubuntu package repositories, in addition to a dedicated Launchpad PPA. Bookworm supports a wide range of ebook and document formats, including EPUB, MOBI, PRC, PDF, and FB2, as well as comic book archive formats CBR and CBZ, making it useful for both text-based ebooks and digital comics. Its library management features let users tag books, edit and search metadata, and switch between grid and list views of their collection, with the ability to edit metadata directly from either view. For reading itself, Bookworm offers three built-in visual profiles, light, sepia, and dark, to reduce eye strain in different lighting conditions, supports bookmarking multiple pages within a book, and includes a full-screen reading mode accessible via the F11 shortcut. As a fully open-source project distributed under a free license, Bookworm has no cost, subscription, or premium tier of any kind; all features are available to every user who installs it. The project is designed primarily for elementary OS, where its interface most closely matches the platform's native design language, though it is packaged and usable on many other Linux distributions through Flatpak, Snap, and native distribution packages. It does not offer native Windows or macOS builds, and unlike more feature-heavy ebook management tools such as Calibre, it deliberately stays focused on a simple, lightweight reading and organization experience rather than offering format conversion, device syncing, or an extensive plugin ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bookworm
paragraphs: Bookworm is a free, open-source ebook manager and reader created by developer Siddhartha Das (GitHub username babluboy). It is built with the Vala programming language along with GTK and WebKit, giving it a native look and feel that is optimized for elementary OS while remaining installable on many other Linux distributions.

The application is distributed through Flathub, Snap packages, Debian and Ubuntu repositories, and a dedicated Launchpad PPA, with source code and documentation hosted on GitHub and at babluboy.github.io/bookworm.

Bookworm focuses on a simple, lightweight reading and library-management experience rather than trying to match the extensive feature set of heavier tools like Calibre, making it a good fit for users who primarily want a clean way to organize and read their existing ebook files on Linux.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bookworm supports EPUB, MOBI, PRC, PDF, and FB2 ebook formats, as well as CBR and CBZ comic archive formats, covering both text-based books and digital comics in a single application. Its library view lets users tag books, edit metadata, and switch between grid and list layouts.

For reading, Bookworm offers light, sepia, and dark visual profiles, bookmarking across multiple pages within a book, and a full-screen reading mode toggled with the F11 key, giving readers a distraction-free way to consume long-form content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bookworm is completely free and open source, with all features available to every user at no cost and no premium or paid tier of any kind.
Because it is open source, users can also inspect, modify, and redistribute the code themselves under the project's open-source license.

features.title: Multi-Format Ebook Support
features.description: Reads EPUB, MOBI, PRC, PDF, and FB2 ebook files in a single application.

features.title: Comic Book Support
features.description: Supports CBR and CBZ comic archive formats alongside standard ebook formats.

features.title: Library Tagging and Metadata Editing
features.description: Lets users tag books and edit metadata for easier searching and filtering.

features.title: Grid and List Library Views
features.description: Switches between grid and list layouts for browsing the ebook collection.

features.title: Light, Sepia, and Dark Reading Themes
features.description: Offers three built-in visual profiles to suit different lighting and reading preferences.

features.title: Page Bookmarking
features.description: Allows bookmarking multiple pages within a single book for quick return.

features.title: Full-Screen Reading Mode
features.description: Provides a distraction-free full-screen mode toggled with the F11 shortcut.

features.title: Native Linux Packaging
features.description: Distributed via Flathub, Snap, and Debian and Ubuntu package repositories for easy installation.

pros: Completely free and open source with no premium tier
Native GTK interface that fits naturally into elementary OS and other GNOME-based desktops
Supports a wide range of formats including comics, not just standard ebooks
Lightweight and focused rather than bloated with unnecessary features
Available through major Linux package channels including Flathub and Snap
cons: Linux-only, with no native Windows or macOS builds
Fewer advanced features than heavier tools like Calibre, such as format conversion
No built-in cloud sync between devices
Smaller community and less frequent updates than mainstream ebook software
Interface is optimized primarily for elementary OS's design language

faqs.question: What is Bookworm?
faqs.answer: Bookworm is a free, open-source ebook manager and reader built with GTK, designed primarily for elementary OS but usable on other Linux distributions.

faqs.question: Is Bookworm free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bookworm is completely free and open source with no paid tier.

faqs.question: What ebook formats does Bookworm support?
faqs.answer: Bookworm supports EPUB, MOBI, PRC, PDF, FB2, and the comic archive formats CBR and CBZ.

faqs.question: Does Bookworm work outside of elementary OS?
faqs.answer: Yes, while optimized for elementary OS, Bookworm is packaged for many Linux distributions via Flathub, Snap, and Debian and Ubuntu repositories.

faqs.question: Who develops Bookworm?
faqs.answer: Bookworm is developed by Siddhartha Das, known on GitHub as babluboy, as an open-source project.

faqs.question: Can Bookworm read comic books?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bookworm supports the CBR and CBZ comic book archive formats alongside standard ebook formats.

faqs.question: How do I install Bookworm?
faqs.answer: Bookworm can be installed via Flathub, Snap packages, or native Debian and Ubuntu package repositories.

faqs.question: Does Bookworm sync ebooks across devices?
faqs.answer: No, Bookworm does not offer native cloud syncing between devices.

use_cases.title: Native ebook reading on Linux
use_cases.description: Linux and elementary OS users install Bookworm as a lightweight, native application for reading their ebook collection.

use_cases.title: Organizing a large multi-format ebook library
use_cases.description: Readers with EPUB, MOBI, and PDF files across a large collection use Bookworm's tagging and metadata tools to keep everything organized.

use_cases.title: Reading digital comics on Linux
use_cases.description: Comic readers use Bookworm's CBR and CBZ support to browse and read digital comic archives on a Linux desktop.

features: Full ebook and comic format support
Light, sepia, and dark reading themes

enrichment.short_description: Boomi is a leading enterprise integration-platform-as-a-service (iPaaS) that connects applications, data, and APIs across cloud and on-premises systems, now owned by private equity firms Francisco Partners and TPG after being spun off from Dell Technologies in 2021.
enrichment.seo_title: Boomi Review 2026: Enterprise iPaaS Integration Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Boomi is a leading iPaaS connecting apps, data, and APIs. Learn its ownership by Francisco Partners and TPG, features, pricing, and possible 2026 IPO plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Boomi is an enterprise integration-platform-as-a-service, commonly called iPaaS, that connects applications, data, and APIs across cloud and on-premises systems for large organizations. The company was founded in 2000 in Berwyn, Pennsylvania, by Rick Nucci and Brian Mozhdehi, and launched its core integration service in 2007. Dell Technologies acquired Boomi in November 2010 and operated it as Dell Boomi. In 2021, Dell sold Boomi to a private equity consortium made up of Francisco Partners and TPG Capital in a deal valued at approximately 4 billion dollars, at which point the company was restructured as an independent entity, Boomi LP, and dropped the Dell name to become simply Boomi. As of the current reporting period, Boomi remains owned by Francisco Partners and TPG Capital. Late-2025 reporting indicated the two private equity owners were evaluating a possible initial public offering in 2026 targeting a valuation in the range of 7 to 9 billion dollars, contingent in part on customer adoption of Boomi's newer AI-driven features, marketed as Boomi GPT, and continued margin expansion under CEO Steve Lucas, whose appointment has been widely read by industry observers as a signal of IPO preparation given his track record scaling and exiting enterprise software companies. Boomi is headquartered in Conshohocken, Pennsylvania, having relocated there from Chesterbrook, Pennsylvania, in early 2024, and employs roughly 2,800 to 2,900 people worldwide as of recent counts, with workforce growth of more than 25 percent between 2023 and 2025. The Boomi platform itself provides a low-code, cloud-native integration process designer connecting SaaS applications, on-premises systems, and cloud infrastructure, alongside API management for designing and securing APIs, master data management for unifying and governing data across systems, B2B and EDI management for trading partner integrations, data preparation tooling, and generative AI-assisted integration building through Boomi GPT. Boomi sells to enterprise customers through custom, quote-based contracts rather than published self-serve pricing, with cost typically depending on connector usage, data volume, and the specific mix of integration, API management, and MDM products a customer adopts.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Boomi
paragraphs: Boomi is an enterprise integration-platform-as-a-service, or iPaaS, that connects applications, data, and APIs across cloud and on-premises environments for large organizations. It was founded in 2000 in Berwyn, Pennsylvania, by Rick Nucci and Brian Mozhdehi, and launched its core integration service in 2007.

Dell Technologies acquired Boomi in November 2010, operating it for a decade as Dell Boomi. In 2021, Dell sold the business to a private equity consortium of Francisco Partners and TPG Capital in a deal valued at roughly 4 billion dollars, and the company was re-established as an independent entity, Boomi LP, dropping the Dell name.

Boomi remains owned by Francisco Partners and TPG Capital today. Late-2025 reporting suggested the owners were weighing a possible 2026 initial public offering targeting a 7 to 9 billion dollar valuation, tied partly to adoption of Boomi's newer AI-driven Boomi GPT features and continued margin growth under CEO Steve Lucas. The company is headquartered in Conshohocken, Pennsylvania, after relocating from Chesterbrook in early 2024, and employs roughly 2,800 to 2,900 people.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Boomi provides a low-code, cloud-native integration process designer for connecting SaaS applications, on-premises systems, and cloud infrastructure without heavy custom coding. It pairs this with API management for designing, publishing, and securing APIs, and a large library of pre-built connectors to common enterprise and SaaS applications.

Boomi also offers master data management to unify and govern data across systems, B2B and EDI management for trading partner integrations, data preparation and transformation tooling, and Boomi GPT, a generative AI assistant that helps build and troubleshoot integration workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Boomi does not publish self-serve pricing. As an enterprise platform, it is sold through custom, quote-based contracts that typically depend on factors such as connector usage, data volume, and the specific mix of integration, API management, MDM, and B2B products a customer licenses.
Prospective customers generally need to engage Boomi's sales team directly to receive pricing tailored to their integration scope and scale.

features.title: Low-Code Integration Process Designer
features.description: A visual, low-code interface for building integration workflows across cloud and on-premises systems.

features.title: API Management
features.description: Tools for designing, publishing, securing, and monitoring APIs.

features.title: Master Data Management (MDM)
features.description: Unifies and governs data consistency across connected systems.

features.title: B2B/EDI Management
features.description: Manages trading partner relationships and electronic data interchange integrations.

features.title: Pre-Built Connector Library
features.description: A large catalog of pre-built connectors to common enterprise and SaaS applications.

features.title: Data Preparation
features.description: Tools for cleansing, transforming, and preparing data as part of integration pipelines.

features.title: Boomi GPT
features.description: A generative AI assistant that helps build, troubleshoot, and optimize integration workflows.

features.title: Cloud-Native Architecture
features.description: Runs as a multi-tenant cloud platform designed to scale across large enterprise integration workloads.

pros: Mature platform with more than two decades of iPaaS market presence
Broad connector ecosystem covering most major enterprise and SaaS applications
Unifies integration, API management, MDM, and EDI in a single suite
Well-capitalized private equity ownership investing in AI-driven features like Boomi GPT
Strong fit for large enterprises with complex, multi-system integration needs
cons: Pricing is not transparent and requires a custom sales quote
Can require specialized skills or training to build complex integration flows
Total cost may be high for small or mid-sized businesses relative to lighter iPaaS tools
Migrating existing integrations from a competing iPaaS platform can be involved
Ownership uncertainty around a potential IPO could affect long-term product direction

faqs.question: Who owns Boomi now?
faqs.answer: Boomi is owned by private equity firms Francisco Partners and TPG Capital, who acquired it from Dell Technologies in 2021 for approximately 4 billion dollars.

faqs.question: Was Boomi part of Dell?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dell Technologies owned Boomi from November 2010 until it was sold to Francisco Partners and TPG Capital in 2021.

faqs.question: Is Boomi going public?
faqs.answer: As of late 2025 reporting, Francisco Partners and TPG were reportedly evaluating a possible 2026 IPO targeting a 7 to 9 billion dollar valuation, though no IPO had been confirmed at the time of this research.

faqs.question: What does Boomi do?
faqs.answer: Boomi is an integration-platform-as-a-service that connects applications, data, and APIs across cloud and on-premises systems, alongside API management, master data management, and B2B/EDI integration.

faqs.question: Where is Boomi headquartered?
faqs.answer: Boomi is headquartered in Conshohocken, Pennsylvania, having relocated there from Chesterbrook in early 2024.

faqs.question: What is Boomi GPT?
faqs.answer: Boomi GPT is a generative AI assistant built into the Boomi platform that helps users build and troubleshoot integration workflows.

faqs.question: How much does Boomi cost?
faqs.answer: Boomi does not publish pricing; it sells through custom, quote-based enterprise contracts based on usage and product mix.

faqs.question: What is Boomi used for?
faqs.answer: Boomi is used by enterprises to integrate cloud and on-premises applications, manage APIs, govern master data, and handle B2B/EDI trading partner integrations.

use_cases.title: Connecting cloud and on-premises applications
use_cases.description: Enterprise IT and integration teams use Boomi to link SaaS applications with legacy on-premises systems without heavy custom coding.

use_cases.title: Managing EDI and trading partner integrations
use_cases.description: Supply chain and B2B teams use Boomi's EDI management to integrate with trading partners and automate document exchange.
use_cases.audience: Supply chain and B2B operations teams

use_cases.title: Building and governing enterprise APIs
use_cases.description: API and platform engineering teams use Boomi to design, secure, and monitor internal and external APIs.

enrichment.short_description: boomurl is a free static site hosting service that turns an uploaded HTML file or folder into a live public URL in about 20 seconds, built especially for publishing AI-generated pages, demos, resumes, and prototypes.
enrichment.starting_price: Free; ad-removal add-on from $2/month per site
enrichment.seo_title: boomurl Review: Instant Free Static Site Hosting
enrichment.seo_meta_description: boomurl turns an HTML file or folder into a live URL in seconds. Free static hosting for AI-generated pages, demos, resumes, and prototypes.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: boomurl, at boomurl.com, is a free static site hosting service created by founder Doron Grinstein and launched in June 2026. Its core function is extremely simple: a user drags in an HTML file, or a folder containing an index.html file, along with any related assets such as Markdown files, PDFs, or plain text documents, picks a site name, verifies ownership via email with no password required, and receives a live public URL in about 20 seconds, with no build process, server configuration, or account setup required. boomurl positions itself explicitly around the rise of AI-generated web content, marketing itself as a fast way to publish pages produced by tools such as ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Cursor, and Replit, as well as more traditional use cases like sharing a resume, a quick demo, a prototype, or an event page. Beyond the core drag-and-drop web interface, boomurl offers developer-facing integrations including API access, a command-line interface tool, support for the Model Context Protocol so AI agents can publish sites programmatically, and an OpenAPI specification for automated integrations. Users can also point their own custom domain at a boomurl-hosted site rather than using boomurl's free subdomain. The free tier allows sites up to 50 megabytes and 500 files, with a small hosted-by-boomurl banner displayed at the bottom of published pages; removing that banner costs 2 dollars per month per site through a prepaid credit system. Because boomurl only hosts static content, it is not designed for dynamic web applications requiring server-side logic, databases, or user authentication; its scope is deliberately limited to fast, no-friction publishing of already-built static files. As a service that launched in mid-2026, boomurl is a very new entrant in the static hosting space, distinguishing itself from more established competitors like Netlify, Vercel, or GitHub Pages primarily through its emphasis on speed, zero-configuration publishing, and first-class support for AI agents and AI-generated content as a direct publishing target.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is boomurl
paragraphs: boomurl, at boomurl.com, is a free static site hosting service that turns an uploaded HTML file or folder into a live public URL in about 20 seconds. It was created by founder Doron Grinstein and launched in June 2026.

The product is built around simplicity: users drag in a file or folder, pick a site name, verify their email with no password required, and get a live link almost immediately, with no build step, server setup, or account onboarding involved.

boomurl markets itself heavily around the rise of AI-generated content, positioning itself as a natural publishing destination for pages produced by tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Cursor, and Replit, in addition to more conventional use cases such as resumes, demos, prototypes, and event pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core publishing supports HTML pages, Markdown files, PDFs, and plain text documents, along with full folder uploads, and each free site is capped at 50 megabytes and 500 files. Security is handled through email verification and IP logging rather than requiring a password or full account setup.

For developers and AI agents, boomurl offers API access, a command-line interface, an OpenAPI specification, and support for the Model Context Protocol, allowing AI tools to publish sites programmatically. Users can also connect a custom domain to a boomurl-hosted site instead of using the default subdomain.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: boomurl is free to use for publishing static sites within its file-size and file-count limits, with a small hosted-by-boomurl banner displayed at the bottom of pages on the free tier.

Removing that banner costs 2 dollars per month per site, purchased through a prepaid credit system, making boomurl one of the lowest-cost static hosting options available for users who want an ad-free published page.

features.title: Drag-and-Drop Publishing
features.description: Upload an HTML file or a folder containing an index.html file and get a live public URL almost instantly.

features.title: Fast, No-Build Deployment
features.description: Sites go live in about 20 seconds with no build process, server configuration, or account setup required.

features.title: Multi-Format Support
features.description: Accepts HTML, Markdown, PDF, and plain text files in addition to full folder uploads.

features.title: AI Agent and Workflow Support
features.description: Built for publishing pages generated by AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Cursor, and Replit.

features.title: Developer Integrations
features.description: Offers API access, a CLI tool, an OpenAPI specification, and Model Context Protocol support for programmatic publishing.

features.title: Custom Domain Support
features.description: Lets users point their own domain name at a boomurl-hosted site.

features.title: Email Verification Security
features.description: Uses email verification and IP logging instead of requiring a password or full account creation.

features.title: Generous Free Tier
features.description: Free sites support up to 50 megabytes of storage and 500 files.

pros: Extremely fast, low-friction publishing with no signup or build process
Genuinely free tier with reasonable file-size and file-count limits
Well suited to publishing AI-agent-generated static pages and demos
Developer-friendly with API, CLI, and Model Context Protocol support
Very low-cost banner removal at 2 dollars per month per site
cons: Very new service with a limited public track record, having launched in mid-2026
Free tier displays a hosted-by-boomurl banner on published pages
Static content only, not suited to dynamic web applications with server-side logic
File size and file count are capped even on paid usage
Smaller ecosystem and community than established static hosts like Netlify or GitHub Pages

faqs.question: What is boomurl?
faqs.answer: boomurl is a free static site hosting service that turns an uploaded HTML file or folder into a live public URL in about 20 seconds.

faqs.question: Is boomurl free?
faqs.answer: Yes, boomurl is free to use within its file-size and file-count limits, with an optional 2 dollar per month per site charge to remove the hosted-by-boomurl banner.

faqs.question: What can I upload to boomurl?
faqs.answer: boomurl accepts HTML files, Markdown files, PDFs, plain text documents, and full folders containing an index.html file.

faqs.question: How fast does a boomurl site go live?
faqs.answer: Sites typically go live in about 20 seconds after upload and email verification.

faqs.question: Can AI agents publish directly to boomurl?
faqs.answer: Yes, boomurl supports the Model Context Protocol along with API and CLI access so AI agents can publish sites programmatically.

faqs.question: Does boomurl support custom domains?
faqs.answer: Yes, users can point their own custom domain at a site hosted on boomurl.

faqs.question: What are boomurl's file limits?
faqs.answer: Free sites on boomurl support up to 50 megabytes of storage and 500 files.

faqs.question: Who created boomurl?
faqs.answer: boomurl was created by founder Doron Grinstein and launched in June 2026.

use_cases.title: Publishing AI-generated prototypes
use_cases.description: Developers and AI agents use boomurl to instantly publish static pages generated by tools like ChatGPT, Claude, or Cursor.

use_cases.title: Hosting a resume or portfolio page
use_cases.description: Job seekers and freelancers use boomurl to quickly publish a resume or simple portfolio page without setting up hosting infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Sharing one-off landing or event pages
use_cases.description: Marketers and founders use boomurl to publish quick landing pages or event pages without involving a full web development workflow.
